entry.title: Roslyn MCP workspace navigation: what it gives an agent
entry.summary: Related files, Cascade-aligned presets, subgraphs, and breakpoint resolution by symbol — why an agent needs a “where am I in the solution?” layer, not just symbols and refactorings.

entry.title: Attention, friction, and neurodivergence in the IDE
entry.summary: A design hypothesis: a stable cockpit-style attention hierarchy can reduce unnecessary friction for many developers—including challenges often reported by neurodivergent engineers. Not medical advice.

entry.title: Equal right to summarize, and why host-side compression is a weak foundation
entry.summary: Explicit summaries and exports are a shared discipline; opaque host compression replaces the verifiable artifact and breaks parity.

entry.title: Knowledge base, trust, and curiosity
entry.summary: How a shared knowledge base became part of the stack, and why provisional trust in the other—human or not—plus curiosity beats defensive blame.

entry.title: Why Cascade IDE borrows a cockpit attention model
entry.summary: Signal overload, context switches, and a deliberate hierarchy of zones — PFD, MFD, EICAS — so the editor stays primary and the agent stays observable.

entry.title: Why this human–agent workspace is Agile in spirit
entry.summary: Short feedback loops, inspect & adapt, cooperation over blame — the same family of ideas as Agile, applied to people and models together.

entry.title: Why Agent-First Learn exists
entry.summary: A short take on the methodology repo: the gap it fills, environment over model, cooperation, and asking “why” instead of moralizing errors.

entry.title: Why these projects, and why parity matters
entry.summary: MCP servers bridge LLMs and real developer tools; parity lets humans and agents share the same ground truth.
